explanation: Push and pop are the two primitives of a stack, a LIFO structure where the last item pushed is the first popped. A queue's primitives are enqueue and dequeue (FIFO).
In classful addressing a Class A address uses only the first octet for the network ID, so the default mask is /8 = 255.0.0.0. 255.255.0.0 (/16) is Class B and 255.255.255.0 (/24) is Class C.
FDDI is built on a dual counter-rotating token ring: two rings running in opposite directions so a break in one can be wrapped onto the other. Bus and star are the Ethernet topologies, not FDDI's.

The physical layer is the only layer that touches the medium: it converts the bit stream into electrical, optical or electromagnetic signals and defines voltages, timing and connectors. Data Link works on frames of bits, not on signals.

The transport layer's job is end-to-end (process-to-process) delivery: it takes the message from the sending process and delivers it to the receiving process, regardless of how many hops lie between. Node-to-node delivery is the data link layer and maintaining routing tables is the network layer, which are the two distractors.

The canonical OSI application-layer service list is network virtual terminal, file transfer/access/management, mail services and directory services, so options A, B and C are each true and 'All of the above' (D) is the only consistent answer. Note the printed answer sheet keys this as C (Mail service); that key is wrong because it singles out one item from a list where all three are correct.

Answer unverified: as printed, options A and D are byte-identical ('Fiber Distributed Data Interface'), so two labels are simultaneously correct and the choice of A over D is positional, not technical. On the content there is no doubt: FDDI expands to Fiber Distributed Data Interface, and B and C are word-swap traps.

Only A is true: a single processor can execute just one process at any given instant. B is false because a multiprogrammed OS preempts and time-slices, so the next process runs long before the current one finishes, and C is false and self-refuting since a single CPU is exactly the case that needs scheduling. Note the printed answer sheet keys D ('All of the above'), which cannot hold once B and C are false.

The blocked (waiting) state holds processes that cannot run until some external event completes, typically an I/O operation. Option C is the trap: a process waiting for the processor is in the Ready state, not blocked, because it needs nothing but the CPU.
The process states are new, ready, running, blocked/waiting and terminated. 'Privileged' is a CPU execution mode (kernel vs user mode), not a state a process sits in.

In a Moore machine the output is a function of the current state alone, so the output is labelled inside the state bubble. Option A describes a Mealy machine, where the output depends on both the current state and the current input.

Running several programs apparently at the same time is multitasking: the OS time-slices the CPU between them so each appears to progress concurrently. 'Multioperating' and 'multipaging' are not standard terms, and Extreme Programming is an agile software development methodology, not an OS concept.
issue: Old text lumped all three distractors together as 'not real OS terms', which is sloppy: Extreme Programming is a real term, just from software process, not operating systems. Rewritten to name each distractor's actual status and to say how multitasking works (time-slicing) rather than just asserting it.

explanation: Big-O is an asymptotic upper bound, so by convention it is used to state the worst case. Omega is the lower bound (best case) and Theta is a tight bound; the trap is assuming Big-O means 'average', which it does not.

F = x + y'z is 1 whenever either term is 1. The x term makes all four rows with x=1 (100, 101, 110, 111) output 1, and y'z makes the single row with y=0 and z=1 output 1, which with x=0 is row 001. Every other row (000, 010, 011) is 0.

Tracing the circuit gives three AND terms fed into an OR: f = x1'x2x3' + x1x2x3' + x1x2x3, which simplifies to x2(x1 + x3'). So f is 1 only on rows 010, 110 and 111, and 0 everywhere else. Note x2 = 1 is required in every product term, so any row with x2 = 0 is immediately 0.
